bibliographic.citation: Eugen Kölbing, 'Die jüngere englische Fassung der Theophilussage, mit einer Einleitung zum ersten Male herausgegeben,' Englische Studien 1 (1877): 16-57; Nachtrag 186; corrections 540.
bibliographic.title: Die jüngere englische Fassung der Theophilussage, mit einer Einleitung zum ersten Male herausgegeben

entities.description: The first publication of this Middle English Theophilus version. Its introduction occupies pp. 16-37 and its parallel H/V text pp. 38-57; a Nachtrag appears on p. 186 and corrections on p. 540.
entities.label: Eugen Kölbing, Die jüngere englische Fassung der Theophilussage (1877)
notes: Kölbing's closing dateline is Breslau, July 1876.
The Nachtrag acknowledges additional Northern Homily Cycle manuscripts and says the edition on pp. 38ff. is not yet a critical edition.

rights.basis: The article was first published in 1877; Internet Archive reports no visible copyright notice and NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the original-volume scan item.
rights.component: 1877 article text and typography

next_steps: Request complete physical objects rather than target folios alone, including covers, binding, spine, edges, flyleaves, blanks, and adjacent works, and identify the exact Theophilus locus separately.
Ask each repository for resolution, price, delivery format, publication, repository redistribution, and commercial print-on-demand terms before reuse.
openQuestions.question: Can complete color physical-object facsimiles be obtained under usable terms for the eleven witnesses without verified complete public digital objects?

next_steps: Compare every italicized or supplied form in the original 1877 print against the mapped Vernon images and a requested complete Harley 4196 facsimile.
Keep any conclusions witness-specific and distinguish abbreviation expansion, supplied loss, normalization, and print emphasis.
openQuestions.question: Exactly which manuscript features do Kölbing's italic letters and other typographic interventions represent in each H and V stream?

next_steps: Review post-2008 Northern Homily Cycle scholarship while retaining the Austin-canon attribution and Carver's 1295-1306 date as hypotheses rather than facts.
openQuestions.question: Can the Theophilus item's composition date and authorship be narrowed independently of disputed cycle-wide hypotheses?

reviewNotes: The abstract medieval work, Kölbing's article, physical witnesses, complete-object facsimiles, later scholarly descriptions, the U-M encoding, and the checked-in XML artifact are distinct entities.
H and V are parallel editorial witness streams, not intrinsic chapters or versions that should be counted together as the work's length.
Caching a source for research does not grant permission to redistribute its images or publish them through print-on-demand services.

lineage.summary: CME00065 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan electronic transcription of Eugen Kölbing's 1877 parallel edition of the Northern Homily Cycle Theophilus legend. Kölbing prints Harley MS 4196 (H) and the Vernon Manuscript (V) in full parallel streams and uses fire-damaged Cotton MS Tiberius E VII (T) selectively to correct and annotate H. A complete original scan of Englische Studien 1, a later photographic-reprint scan, the complete 710-canvas Vernon codex, and the complete 290-canvas Lambeth Palace Library MS 260 are cached. Exact work loci are mapped inside the complete manuscript bundles. Huntington HM 129 exposes only fourteen selected images with no overlap at the Theophilus locus, so that cache is explicitly partial and has no work-portion mapping. DIMEV's current fourteen-witness census is recorded separately from the three witnesses Kölbing actually used.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Northern Homily Cycle poet
catalogSummary.date: Probably around 1300 or the early fourteenth century; earliest surviving exact witness identified here dated 1340-1360; first published in 1877
catalogSummary.region: Northern England, perhaps Yorkshire; surviving copies span northern, Midland, southern, and Irish transmission
catalogSummary.title: The Legend of Theophilus (Northern Homily Cycle)

catalogingSubject.label: The Legend of Theophilus (Northern Homily Cycle)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The single anonymous Middle English verse exemplum about Theophilus as transmitted within the Northern Homily Cycle and represented by CME00065. It is not the complete Northern Homily Cycle, Kölbing's entire 1877 German article, either physical manuscript, a two-part work, the U-M encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact. Kölbing's H and V texts are parallel witness representations of one work.

contentParts.label: H witness stream (Harley MS 4196)
notes: Representation-only part: it is not an intrinsic chapter or a separately authored work.
T at ff. 221rb-225rb contributes selected variants and corrections but not another continuous encoded stream.
contentParts.summary: Kölbing's complete H text, selectively corrected and annotated from the closely related, fire-damaged T witness.

contentParts.label: V witness stream (Vernon Manuscript)

locators.value: second DIV2, V stream; 726 L elements

notes: Representation-only part: it is not an intrinsic chapter or a separately authored work.
The metrics report XML elements. Printed V numbering ends at 725; the extra XML L element reflects an encoding/numbering offset and is not evidence of a 726-line authorial text.
contentParts.summary: Kölbing's complete V text, preserving a substantially divergent recension that the editor prints parallel to H.

assertion.notes: The range conservatively represents current cycle-level dating and should not be mistaken for a directly dated authorial copy.

dateStatements.display: Probably around 1300 or in the early fourteenth century; the cycle-wide 1295-1306 proposal is not definitive for this item

assertion.notes: Earliest surviving exact witness identified in the present fourteen-witness census, not necessarily the date of composition.

dateStatements.display: 1340-1360, in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Ashmole 42

notes: Kölbing's title explicitly describes the version as published for the first time.

extent.description: One verse exemplum. No single intrinsic line count is asserted because Kölbing and CME00065 present two divergent complete witness streams: H has 798 numbered lines; V has 725 numbered lines. The XML contains 1,524 L elements (798 H plus 726 V elements), one more V element than the printed V numbering because an encoding position offset appears around the N=550 milestone. The XML's 56 LG groups are also representation-level, not a stable abstract-work stanza count.

formStatements.description: Octosyllabic rhyming couplets; DIMEV describes the versification as two-line aa.

genres.label: Sermon exempla
notes: The tale is embedded in the Northern Homily Cycle's thirteenth-Sunday-after-Trinity homily in H.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: German exposition and Latin rubrics belong to Kölbing's edition or witness paratext, not to the abstract work's primary language statement.
The preferred English title is supplied for discovery and disambiguation; witnesses preserve several different rubrics and some none.
The DIMEV witness census is current collateral evidence. Only H, T, and V are documented inputs to Kölbing's 1877 edition.
German and Latin are edition or paratext languages and therefore are not included in the abstract work's language_codes.
The anti-Jewish stereotype subject note is included to support responsible discovery and contextualization of harmful narrative content.
Whole-manuscript facsimile availability, exact target mappings, cache state, and image-use rights belong to the linked lineage manifest rather than being collapsed into descriptive work metadata.

next_steps: Review post-2008 scholarship on the Northern Homily Cycle and the Carver 1295-1306 proposal, recording competing ranges separately.
openQuestions.question: Can this Theophilus item's composition date be narrowed independently of cycle-wide proposals?

next_steps: Treat the cycle poet as anonymous unless a directly supported attribution emerges; do not convert institutional milieu into a named author.
openQuestions.question: Does later scholarship materially strengthen or weaken the proposed Austin-canon authorship context for this item?

next_steps: Collate the V XML against printed pp. 38-57 and Vernon canvases 294-297, then correct the encoding only in a separately authorized change; retain witness-specific counts in the meantime.
openQuestions.question: Should an abstract-work line count ever be asserted across the divergent H and V recensions, and how should the one-element V XML numbering offset be corrected?

notes: Kölbing argues from rhyme that the poem is northern and that the Vernon scribe southernized forms.

assertion.notes: Yorkshire is a cycle-wide scholarly possibility, not a securely localized composition place for this item.

notes: Do not assign the work to Richard Rolle: Kölbing says resemblance to Prick of Conscience does not establish the same author.

scope.description: Complete H and V witness streams

notes: T is represented by corrections and selected variants, not by a third continuous stream.

scope.description: Selective comparison of the closely related T witness against H

subjects.label: Clerical ambition and a pact with the devil
Anti-Jewish sorcerer stereotype
notes: The tale uses a Jewish magician as the intermediary in Theophilus's pact with the devil. This subject heading records harmful narrative content rather than endorsing it.

summaries.value: Theophilus, a virtuous episcopal official, refuses elevation and later loses his office. Driven by wounded status and despair, he seeks a Jewish magician, renounces Christ and the Virgin, and signs a pact with the devil. Repenting, he undertakes penance and prays to Mary, who intercedes for him and restores the written pact. After public confession and communion, he dies reconciled. The Northern Homily Cycle uses the narrative as an exemplum of pride, sin, penance, confession, Marian intercession, mercy, and salvation.

CME00065 serializes Kölbing's H stream and V stream as two sequential divisions because XML cannot reproduce the printed parallel columns directly. H has 798 numbered lines; V has 725 printed line numbers. These are manuscript-witness representations of one tale and must not be combined into an intrinsic 1,523- or 1,524-line work extent.

Kölbing knew three copies in 1877 and directly used H, T, and V. DIMEV now lists fourteen witnesses. The eleven additional copies are collateral modern-census evidence, not retroactive sources of Kölbing's edition. Kölbing argues that H/T and V descend through divergent classes from a common source but does not claim that either derives directly from the other or that the common source can be reconstructed.

titles.supplied_by: General Corpus, following DIMEV's work identification while distinguishing this version from other Theophilus legends

work.summary: Theophilus, a virtuous episcopal official, refuses elevation and later loses his office. Driven by wounded status and despair, he seeks a Jewish magician, renounces Christ and the Virgin, and signs a pact with the devil. Repenting, he undertakes penance and prays to Mary, who intercedes for him and restores the written pact. After public confession and communion, he dies reconciled. The Northern Homily Cycle uses the narrative as an exemplum of pride, sin, penance, confession, Marian intercession, mercy, and salvation.
